# Lineage integration options for DataQ — research report

**Premise:** DataQ pulls lineage from external systems (for blast-radius questions like "which downstream tables are affected by this failing check?") and emits OpenLineage events (so external catalogs can display DataQ's quality results), rather than building its own lineage graph. Constraints: MIT-licensed project (no GPL/AGPL/SSPL/BUSL/Elastic/source-available deps per ADR 0031), vendor-neutral, local-first (docker-compose reference stack), small team.

---

## 1. OpenLineage emission from DataQ (`openlineage-python`)

**License — Apache-2.0, clean.** The OpenLineage repo LICENSE is Apache-2.0 ([github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/LICENSE](https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/LICENSE)) and PyPI declares `Apache-2.0` for `openlineage-python`, latest **1.51.0 released 2026-07-06** — actively maintained ([pypi.org/project/openlineage-python](https://pypi.org/project/openlineage-python/)). The project is an LF AI & Data **Graduate** project (since Sept 2023) ([lfaidata.foundation blog](https://lfaidata.foundation/blog/2023/09/20/lf-ai-data-foundation-announces-graduation-of-openlineage-project/)). Fully compatible with DataQ's MIT policy.

**Transports.** HTTP (retry + compression), Console (the default when unconfigured), File (local or S3/GCS/Azure via the `[fsspec]` extra), Kafka (`[kafka]` extra), plus Composite, Transform, Async-HTTP, and custom transports. Config via constructor, `openlineage.yml`, or env vars — legacy `OPENLINEAGE_URL`/`OPENLINEAGE_API_KEY` or the namespaced `OPENLINEAGE__TRANSPORT__TYPE=http` style; `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` is the global kill switch ([openlineage.io/docs/client/python/configuration](https://openlineage.io/docs/client/python/configuration)). The Console default + `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` means DataQ can ship the emitter dark, off-by-default, with zero infra dependency.

**Event model.** `RunEvent` = one observation of a `Run` (UUID `runId`) of a `Job` (namespace + name) with `inputs`/`outputs` `Dataset` lists; `eventType` ∈ START/RUNNING/COMPLETE/ABORT/FAIL/OTHER — the spec expects exactly one START and one terminal event per run ([openlineage.io/docs/spec/object-model](https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/object-model), schema: [spec/OpenLineage.json](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/main/spec/OpenLineage.json)).

**Effort to emit "DQ suite run X read dataset Y" — low, verified code shape** ([openlineage.io/docs/client/python/usage](https://openlineage.io/docs/client/python/usage)):

```python
from openlineage.client import OpenLineageClient
from openlineage.client.event_v2 import Dataset, Job, Run, RunEvent, RunState
from openlineage.client.uuid import generate_new_uuid

client = OpenLineageClient()  # config from env/openlineage.yml
job = Job(namespace="dataq", name="suite.retail_orders")
run = Run(runId=str(generate_new_uuid()))
client.emit(RunEvent(eventType=RunState.START, eventTime=..., run=run, job=job, producer="dataq"))
client.emit(RunEvent(eventType=RunState.COMPLETE, eventTime=..., run=run, job=job, producer="dataq",
                     inputs=[Dataset(namespace="snowflake://acct", name="RETAIL.ORDERS")]))
```

This maps 1:1 onto DataQ's existing run lifecycle (`runs` table start/complete/error) — the emit hooks belong in `run_service`. The only design work is the **dataset naming convention** (OpenLineage namespace/name per datasource type, e.g. `snowflake://<account>` + `db.schema.table`), which the spec standardizes per platform.

**Quality facet — purpose-built for DataQ's use case.** `DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet` (facet key `dataQualityAssertions`, schema [openlineage.io/spec/facets/1-1-0/DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet.json](https://openlineage.io/spec/facets/1-1-0/DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet.json)) attaches an `assertions[]` array to an input dataset: required `assertion` (e.g. `not_null`, `row_count`) + `success` (bool); optional `column`, `name`, `severity` (`error`|`warn` — maps directly to DataQ's severity tiers), `description`, `expected`, `actual` ([facet docs](https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/facets/dataset-facets/data_quality_assertions)). dbt tests and the Great Expectations integration (`OpenLineageValidationAction` on a GX checkpoint) already populate it ([GX integration docs](https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/great-expectations/)) — so DataQ emitting it from its own GX results is idiomatic, not novel. The older `DataQualityMetricsInputDatasetFacet` (rowCount, per-column nullCount/distinctCount/min/max) is a natural carrier for DataQ's `metric_value` monitor-kinds (freshness/volume).

## 2. Marquez (reference consumer)

- **License:** Apache-2.0 ([LICENSE](https://github.com/MarquezProject/marquez/blob/main/LICENSE)). LF AI & Data **Graduated** project ([lfaidata.foundation/projects/marquez](https://lfaidata.foundation/projects/marquez/)).
- **Lineage pull API — exactly the shape DataQ needs:** `GET /api/v1/lineage?nodeId=dataset:<namespace>:<name>&depth=<n>` (depth defaults to 20; nodeId also accepts `job:` and `datasetField:` forms) returns a `graph` of nodes (`type: JOB|DATASET`, `inEdges`, `outEdges`) — a ready-made blast-radius query ([marquezproject.ai/docs/api/get-lineage](https://marquezproject.ai/docs/api/get-lineage/), [openapi.yml](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MarquezProject/marquez/main/spec/openapi.yml)).
- **Ingestion:** native OpenLineage receiver — `POST /api/v1/lineage` accepts RunEvents directly; Marquez is the OpenLineage reference implementation ([record-lineage docs](https://marquezproject.ai/docs/api/record-lineage/)).
- **Deployment weight — light by catalog standards:** two containers (Java/Dropwizard API + `postgres:14`), optional React web UI third; official [docker-compose.yml](https://github.com/MarquezProject/marquez/blob/main/docker-compose.yml) + Helm chart in-repo. No embedded/in-memory mode exists (absence-of-evidence — no doc states it, none found), so Postgres is mandatory — but DataQ's compose stack already runs Postgres.
- **Maintenance caveat:** repo is active (commits into 2026-04, push 2026-07-06) but the **last tagged release is 0.50.0 from 2024-10-24** ([releases](https://github.com/MarquezProject/marquez/releases)) — a stalled release cadence worth noting, though as a dev-time reference consumer (not a runtime dependency) the risk is low.

## 3. DataHub

- **License:** core Apache-2.0 ([LICENSE](https://github.com/datahub-project/datahub/blob/master/LICENSE)); Python SDK `acryl-datahub` also Apache-2.0 ([pypi.org/project/acryl-datahub](https://pypi.org/project/acryl-datahub/)). **No licensing controversy found** despite targeted search — the one soft flag is the 2025 Acryl Data → "DataHub" corporate rebrand + "DataHub Cloud" paid product, creating OSS/vendor/product name overlap ([Series B announcement](https://datahub.com/news/series-b-announcement/), [TechCrunch on Acryl](https://techcrunch.com/2021/06/23/acryl-data-commercializing-linkedins-metadata-tool-datahub-emerges-from-stealth-with-9m-from-8vc-linkedin-and-insight/)).
- **Lineage read:** GraphQL `dataset(urn:...)` + `lineage(input: {direction: UPSTREAM|DOWNSTREAM})`, multi-hop via `scrollAcrossLineage`; OpenAPI v3 supports lineage PATCH edits ([lineage tutorial](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/api/tutorials/lineage), [OpenAPI guide](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/api/openapi/openapi-usage-guide)).
- **Assertions push — the strongest external-DQ model of the three catalogs:** Assertion entities with `source.type = EXTERNAL` + timeseries `AssertionRunEvent` (pass/fail + severity per run), pushed via `DatahubRestEmitter` or the `upsertCustomAssertion` GraphQL mutation ([custom-assertions tutorial](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/api/tutorials/custom-assertions), [assertion metamodel](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/generated/metamodel/entities/assertion)).
- **OpenLineage ingestion — native HTTP receiver:** `POST <GMS>/openapi/openlineage/api/v1/lineage` accepts OpenLineage events directly ([docs.datahub.com/docs/lineage/openlineage](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/lineage/openlineage)) — DataQ's emitter reaches DataHub with zero extra code.
- **Deployment weight — heavy:** quickstart deploys Kafka + Zookeeper + MySQL + OpenSearch + GMS + frontend + Actions; documented minimum 2 CPU / 8 GB RAM / 13 GB disk ([quickstart](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/quickstart)). Too heavy for DataQ's local-first compose reference stack.
- **Maturity:** very active — v1.6.0 shipped 2026-05-21 ([releases](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/releases)).

## 4. OpenMetadata

- **License — the disqualifier.** The server core is Apache-2.0 ([LICENSE](https://github.com/open-metadata/OpenMetadata/blob/main/LICENSE)), **but the Python SDK/ingestion framework `openmetadata-ingestion` — the surface DataQ would actually integrate through — is under the "Collate Community License Agreement, Version 1.0"** ([pypi.org/project/openmetadata-ingestion](https://pypi.org/project/openmetadata-ingestion/), [ingestion/LICENSE](https://github.com/open-metadata/OpenMetadata/blob/main/ingestion/LICENSE)). I independently verified the license text: it contains an "Excluded Purpose" non-compete — *"'Excluded Purpose' means making available any software-as-a-service, platform-as-a-service, infrastructure-as-a-service or other similar online service that competes with Collate products or services."* That is exactly the BUSL/Elastic-style source-available category DataQ's policy prohibits (CLAUDE.md §11, ADR 0031). The UI carries the same license ([openmetadata-ui/LICENSE](https://github.com/open-metadata/OpenMetadata/blob/main/openmetadata-ui/LICENSE)). Talking to the Apache-2.0 REST API directly (no SDK dependency) would technically stay clean, but the license posture is a standing risk.
- **APIs (for completeness):** lineage `GET /api/v1/lineage/<entityType>/name/<fqn>?upstreamDepth=&downstreamDepth=` ([API reference](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/api-reference/lineage)); DQ push `PUT /api/v1/dataQuality/testCases/{fqn}/testCaseResult` ([custom-tests guide](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/data-quality-observability/quality/custom-tests)) — genuinely good DQ-native APIs.
- **OpenLineage support:** exists but **beta and Kafka/Kinesis-only** — no plain HTTP receiver; requires entities pre-registered ([OpenLineage connector docs](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/connectors/pipeline/openlineage)).
- **Deployment weight — heavy:** MySQL/Postgres + Elasticsearch + bundled Airflow; stated minimum 6 GiB RAM / 4 vCPU ([local Docker deployment](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/quick-start/local-docker-deployment)). Active project (1.13.1, 2026-06-27; Collate-backed).

## 5. Microsoft Purview (low priority — Azure wind-down)

- **Lineage read:** Apache Atlas-compatible API — `GET {endpoint}/datamap/api/atlas/v2/lineage/{guid}?direction=BOTH`; Microsoft's FAQ states Atlas compatibility explicitly ([lineage REST API](https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/data-gov-api-create-lineage-relationships), [FAQ](https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/data-governance-faq)).
- **OpenLineage status:** no general-purpose native receiver. Airflow lineage is public **preview** via a bring-your-own Event Hub + `openlineage-airflow` Kafka transport, limited Airflow versions/sources, no column lineage ([Airflow lineage preview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/purview/data-map-lineage-airflow)); the Databricks path is a 2022 solution accelerator ([Purview-ADB-Lineage-Solution-Accelerator](https://github.com/microsoft/Purview-ADB-Lineage-Solution-Accelerator)). Roadmap language only, not GA.
- **Azure-only:** Purview is an Azure/Fabric-hosted service requiring an Azure subscription ([account creation docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/create-microsoft-purview-portal)). Given DataQ's Azure wind-down (~2026-07-25), this belongs behind a future adapter seam, not in v-next scope.

## 6. Orchestrators as direct pull sources

- **Airflow:** `apache-airflow-providers-openlineage` is the official ASF provider (in-repo since Airflow 2.7, superseding community `openlineage-airflow`), Apache-2.0, auto-emits per-task RunEvents via listener hooks; latest **2.18.1 (2026-06-22)**, 2.19.0rc1 already cut — healthy cadence ([provider docs](https://airflow.apache.org/docs/apache-airflow-providers-openlineage/stable/index.html), [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/apache-airflow-providers-openlineage/)). Key implication: DataQ doesn't "pull from Airflow" — the user configures the provider to **push into whatever OpenLineage receiver DataQ points it at**. This mirrors DataQ's existing Airflow-callback pattern (user-deployed snippet, `integrations/airflow/`).
- **dbt:** still wrapper-based — `dbt-ol run` (package `openlineage-dbt`, Apache-2.0, **1.51.0, 2026-07-06** — versioned with the monorepo, actively maintained) parses `manifest.json` + `run_results.json` post-run and emits events ([integration docs](https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/), [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/openlineage-dbt/)). **No first-party dbt Labs support has shipped**; the feature request [dbt-core#11750](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/11750) (opened 2025-06-18) is open with no maintainer commitment. Notably, DataQ's existing dbt provider (ADR 0029) already polls those same artifacts — the manifest's node graph is a lineage source DataQ already has plumbing for.

---

## Recommended first slice (small team, local-first, MIT-safe)

### (b) Quality-facet publishing — build this FIRST

1. **Emit OpenLineage RunEvents with `DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet` from `run_service`** using `openlineage-python` (Apache-2.0, one pip dep, zero infra). START on run dispatch; COMPLETE/FAIL with the suite's target as an input `Dataset` carrying the assertions facet (check name → `assertion`, pass/fail → `success`, DataQ severity → `severity`, observed/expected → `actual`/`expected`; `metric_value` monitors → `DataQualityMetricsInputDatasetFacet`). Config via standard `OPENLINEAGE_URL`/`OPENLINEAGE__TRANSPORT__*` env vars, console default, `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` honored — off-by-default, no new required service. This one emitter reaches Marquez, DataHub (native HTTP OL receiver), and Kafka consumers **without any per-catalog code**, which is the whole vendor-neutrality argument.
2. **Add Marquez to the reference docker-compose as the demo consumer** (2 containers; reuses the Postgres pattern already in the stack). Apache-2.0, purpose-built OL receiver, and it doubles as the verification harness for slice 1.

*Why first:* lowest effort (days, not weeks — the hooks map 1:1 to the existing run lifecycle), zero license risk, zero mandatory infra, and it makes DataQ a good citizen of the ecosystem before asking anything of it.

### (a) Blast-radius lineage pull — build SECOND, behind a seam

3. **A `LineageProvider` abstraction (mirroring `OrchestrationProvider`) with Marquez as the reference impl:** `get_lineage(dataset) → graph` backed by `GET /api/v1/lineage?nodeId=dataset:<ns>:<name>&depth=N`. Local-first story: Airflow's OL provider and `dbt-ol` push into Marquez; DataQ pulls the graph back out to answer "check failed on X → what's downstream?". Note Marquez's stalled release tags (0.50.0, Oct 2024) in the ADR as an accepted risk.
4. **A dbt-manifest lineage impl as the zero-infra fallback:** DataQ's ADR-0029 dbt provider already polls `manifest.json`/`run_results.json`; parsing the manifest's DAG gives model-level blast radius with **no lineage backend at all** — the true local-first floor.
5. **DataHub impl of the same seam — deferred:** best-in-class external-assertion push API and a native OL HTTP receiver (so slice 1 already works against it), Apache-2.0 SDK; but the Kafka+OpenSearch+MySQL footprint (8 GB RAM minimum) disqualifies it from the compose reference stack. Add the GraphQL lineage-pull impl only when a user brings an existing DataHub.
6. **OpenMetadata — do not integrate via SDK:** the `openmetadata-ingestion` package's Collate Community License (source-available, non-compete) violates DataQ's ADR-0031 policy. If ever needed, use the Apache-2.0 server REST API directly and record the decision in an ADR.
7. **Purview — park it:** Atlas-API read is feasible later behind the same `LineageProvider` seam, but preview-grade OL support + Azure-only hosting + DataQ's Azure wind-down make it last.

**Bottom line:** the emitter-plus-facet slice (openlineage-python → any receiver) is the highest-leverage, license-cleanest first move; Marquez is the right local-first reference consumer despite its slow release tagging; DataHub is the "bring your own catalog" target that works with the same emitter; OpenMetadata is blocked at the SDK layer by a source-available license; Purview is a deferred Azure-only adapter.</result>

- **License:** core Apache-2.0 ([LICENSE](https://github.com/datahub-project/datahub/blob/master/LICENSE)); Python SDK `acryl-datahub` also Apache-2.0 ([pypi.org/project/acryl-datahub](https://pypi.org/project/acryl-datahub/)). **No licensing controversy found** despite targeted search — the one soft flag is the 2025 Acryl Data → "DataHub" corporate rebrand + "DataHub Cloud" paid product, creating OSS/vendor/product name overlap ([Series B announcement](https://datahub.com/news/series-b-announcement/), [TechCrunch on Acryl](https://techcrunch.com/2021/06/23/acryl-data-commercializing-linkedins-metadata-tool-datahub-emerges-from-stealth-with-9m-from-8vc-linkedin-and-insight/)).
- **Lineage read:** GraphQL `dataset(urn:...)` + `lineage(input: {direction: UPSTREAM|DOWNSTREAM})`, multi-hop via `scrollAcrossLineage`; OpenAPI v3 supports lineage PATCH edits ([lineage tutorial](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/api/tutorials/lineage), [OpenAPI guide](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/api/openapi/openapi-usage-guide)).
- **Assertions push — the strongest external-DQ model of the three catalogs:** Assertion entities with `source.type = EXTERNAL` + timeseries `AssertionRunEvent` (pass/fail + severity per run), pushed via `DatahubRestEmitter` or the `upsertCustomAssertion` GraphQL mutation ([custom-assertions tutorial](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/api/tutorials/custom-assertions), [assertion metamodel](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/generated/metamodel/entities/assertion)).
- **OpenLineage ingestion — native HTTP receiver:** `POST <GMS>/openapi/openlineage/api/v1/lineage` accepts OpenLineage events directly ([docs.datahub.com/docs/lineage/openlineage](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/lineage/openlineage)) — DataQ's emitter reaches DataHub with zero extra code.
- **Deployment weight — heavy:** quickstart deploys Kafka + Zookeeper + MySQL + OpenSearch + GMS + frontend + Actions; documented minimum 2 CPU / 8 GB RAM / 13 GB disk ([quickstart](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/quickstart)). Too heavy for DataQ's local-first compose reference stack.
- **Maturity:** very active — v1.6.0 shipped 2026-05-21 ([releases](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/releases)).

## 4. OpenMetadata

- **License — the disqualifier.** The server core is Apache-2.0 ([LICENSE](https://github.com/open-metadata/OpenMetadata/blob/main/LICENSE)), **but the Python SDK/ingestion framework `openmetadata-ingestion` — the surface DataQ would actually integrate through — is under the "Collate Community License Agreement, Version 1.0"** ([pypi.org/project/openmetadata-ingestion](https://pypi.org/project/openmetadata-ingestion/), [ingestion/LICENSE](https://github.com/open-metadata/OpenMetadata/blob/main/ingestion/LICENSE)). I independently verified the license text: it contains an "Excluded Purpose" non-compete — *"'Excluded Purpose' means making available any software-as-a-service, platform-as-a-service, infrastructure-as-a-service or other similar online service that competes with Collate products or services."* That is exactly the BUSL/Elastic-style source-available category DataQ's policy prohibits (CLAUDE.md §11, ADR 0031). The UI carries the same license ([openmetadata-ui/LICENSE](https://github.com/open-metadata/OpenMetadata/blob/main/openmetadata-ui/LICENSE)). Talking to the Apache-2.0 REST API directly (no SDK dependency) would technically stay clean, but the license posture is a standing risk.
- **APIs (for completeness):** lineage `GET /api/v1/lineage/<entityType>/name/<fqn>?upstreamDepth=&downstreamDepth=` ([API reference](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/api-reference/lineage)); DQ push `PUT /api/v1/dataQuality/testCases/{fqn}/testCaseResult` ([custom-tests guide](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/data-quality-observability/quality/custom-tests)) — genuinely good DQ-native APIs.
- **OpenLineage support:** exists but **beta and Kafka/Kinesis-only** — no plain HTTP receiver; requires entities pre-registered ([OpenLineage connector docs](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/connectors/pipeline/openlineage)).
- **Deployment weight — heavy:** MySQL/Postgres + Elasticsearch + bundled Airflow; stated minimum 6 GiB RAM / 4 vCPU ([local Docker deployment](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/quick-start/local-docker-deployment)). Active project (1.13.1, 2026-06-27; Collate-backed).

## 5. Microsoft Purview (low priority — Azure wind-down)

- **Lineage read:** Apache Atlas-compatible API — `GET {endpoint}/datamap/api/atlas/v2/lineage/{guid}?direction=BOTH`; Microsoft's FAQ states Atlas compatibility explicitly ([lineage REST API](https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/data-gov-api-create-lineage-relationships), [FAQ](https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/data-governance-faq)).
- **OpenLineage status:** no general-purpose native receiver. Airflow lineage is public **preview** via a bring-your-own Event Hub + `openlineage-airflow` Kafka transport, limited Airflow versions/sources, no column lineage ([Airflow lineage preview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/purview/data-map-lineage-airflow)); the Databricks path is a 2022 solution accelerator ([Purview-ADB-Lineage-Solution-Accelerator](https://github.com/microsoft/Purview-ADB-Lineage-Solution-Accelerator)). Roadmap language only, not GA.
- **Azure-only:** Purview is an Azure/Fabric-hosted service requiring an Azure subscription ([account creation docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/create-microsoft-purview-portal)). Given DataQ's Azure wind-down (~2026-07-25), this belongs behind a future adapter seam, not in v-next scope.

## 6. Orchestrators as direct pull sources

- **Airflow:** `apache-airflow-providers-openlineage` is the official ASF provider (in-repo since Airflow 2.7, superseding community `openlineage-airflow`), Apache-2.0, auto-emits per-task RunEvents via listener hooks; latest **2.18.1 (2026-06-22)**, 2.19.0rc1 already cut — healthy cadence ([provider docs](https://airflow.apache.org/docs/apache-airflow-providers-openlineage/stable/index.html), [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/apache-airflow-providers-openlineage/)). Key implication: DataQ doesn't "pull from Airflow" — the user configures the provider to **push into whatever OpenLineage receiver DataQ points it at**. This mirrors DataQ's existing Airflow-callback pattern (user-deployed snippet, `integrations/airflow/`).
- **dbt:** still wrapper-based — `dbt-ol run` (package `openlineage-dbt`, Apache-2.0, **1.51.0, 2026-07-06** — versioned with the monorepo, actively maintained) parses `manifest.json` + `run_results.json` post-run and emits events ([integration docs](https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/), [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/openlineage-dbt/)). **No first-party dbt Labs support has shipped**; the feature request [dbt-core#11750](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/11750) (opened 2025-06-18) is open with no maintainer commitment. Notably, DataQ's existing dbt provider (ADR 0029) already polls those same artifacts — the manifest's node graph is a lineage source DataQ already has plumbing for.

---

## Recommended first slice (small team, local-first, MIT-safe)

### (b) Quality-facet publishing — build this FIRST

1. **Emit OpenLineage RunEvents with `DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet` from `run_service`** using `openlineage-python` (Apache-2.0, one pip dep, zero infra). START on run dispatch; COMPLETE/FAIL with the suite's target as an input `Dataset` carrying the assertions facet (check name → `assertion`, pass/fail → `success`, DataQ severity → `severity`, observed/expected → `actual`/`expected`; `metric_value` monitors → `DataQualityMetricsInputDatasetFacet`). Config via standard `OPENLINEAGE_URL`/`OPENLINEAGE__TRANSPORT__*` env vars, console default, `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` honored — off-by-default, no new required service. This one emitter reaches Marquez, DataHub (native HTTP OL receiver), and Kafka consumers **without any per-catalog code**, which is the whole vendor-neutrality argument.
2. **Add Marquez to the reference docker-compose as the demo consumer** (2 containers; reuses the Postgres pattern already in the stack). Apache-2.0, purpose-built OL receiver, and it doubles as the verification harness for slice 1.

*Why first:* lowest effort (days, not weeks — the hooks map 1:1 to the existing run lifecycle), zero license risk, zero mandatory infra, and it makes DataQ a good citizen of the ecosystem before asking anything of it.

### (a) Blast-radius lineage pull — build SECOND, behind a seam

3. **A `LineageProvider` abstraction (mirroring `OrchestrationProvider`) with Marquez as the reference impl:** `get_lineage(dataset) → graph` backed by `GET /api/v1/lineage?nodeId=dataset:<ns>:<name>&depth=N`. Local-first story: Airflow's OL provider and `dbt-ol` push into Marquez; DataQ pulls the graph back out to answer "check failed on X → what's downstream?". Note Marquez's stalled release tags (0.50.0, Oct 2024) in the ADR as an accepted risk.
4. **A dbt-manifest lineage impl as the zero-infra fallback:** DataQ's ADR-0029 dbt provider already polls `manifest.json`/`run_results.json`; parsing the manifest's DAG gives model-level blast radius with **no lineage backend at all** — the true local-first floor.
5. **DataHub impl of the same seam — deferred:** best-in-class external-assertion push API and a native OL HTTP receiver (so slice 1 already works against it), Apache-2.0 SDK; but the Kafka+OpenSearch+MySQL footprint (8 GB RAM minimum) disqualifies it from the compose reference stack. Add the GraphQL lineage-pull impl only when a user brings an existing DataHub.
6. **OpenMetadata — do not integrate via SDK:** the `openmetadata-ingestion` package's Collate Community License (source-available, non-compete) violates DataQ's ADR-0031 policy. If ever needed, use the Apache-2.0 server REST API directly and record the decision in an ADR.
7. **Purview — park it:** Atlas-API read is feasible later behind the same `LineageProvider` seam, but preview-grade OL support + Azure-only hosting + DataQ's Azure wind-down make it last.

**Bottom line:** the emitter-plus-facet slice (openlineage-python → any receiver) is the highest-leverage, license-cleanest first move; Marquez is the right local-first reference consumer despite its slow release tagging; DataHub is the "bring your own catalog" target that works with the same emitter; OpenMetadata is blocked at the SDK layer by a source-available license; Purview is a deferred Azure-only adapter.</result>

The catalog-comparison report is in with a decisive picture: emit OpenLineage first (`openlineage-python` is Apache-2.0, has a purpose-built `DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet`, off-by-default console/disabled modes), Marquez as the local-first reference consumer, dbt-manifest parsing as the zero-infra blast-radius floor, DataHub as bring-your-own-catalog — and one important licensing find: **OpenMetadata's Python SDK is under the Collate source-available license (non-compete clause), which ADR 0031 prohibits**, so it's REST-API-only if ever.

One agent left (OpenLineage naming spec), then I draft.

<result># OpenLineage Dataset Naming Specification — Research Report

**Primary source note:** the canonical naming spec lives at `website/docs/spec/naming.md` in the OpenLineage repo, rendered at **https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming**. The old `spec/Naming.md` file you pointed at is explicitly marked obsolete — I fetched it directly and its entire content is now just a pointer:

> "This document is obsolete, please look at the [OpenLineage Naming Conventions](https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming)"
> — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/Naming.md

All namespace/name templates below are taken verbatim from the current spec table, then cross-checked against the actual reference implementations (`client/python/src/openlineage/client/naming/dataset.py` and `client/java/.../dataset/Naming.java`), which are attr/Builder classes whose `get_namespace()`/`get_name()` implementations are byte-identical to the spec table — i.e., these two files *are* the spec, expressed as code.

---

## 1–2. Per-datasource namespace + name construction, with case/quoting/defaults notes

| Datasource | Namespace template | Name template | Concrete example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Snowflake** | `snowflake://{organization_name}-{account_name}` (preferred, orgname-account format) **or** `snowflake://{account_locator}(.{region}).{cloud}` (legacy locator format) | `{database}.{schema}.{table}` | `snowflake://myorg-myaccount` / `MYDB.PUBLIC.ORDERS` — legacy form: `snowflake://xy12345.us-east-2.aws` / `MYDB.PUBLIC.ORDERS` |
| **Databricks Unity Catalog** | `unitycatalog://{host}` or `unitycatalog://{host}:{port}` | `{catalog}.{schema}.{table}` | `unitycatalog://adb-123456789.0.azuredatabricks.net` / `main.sales.orders` |
| **ADLS Gen2 (abfss://)** | `abfss://{container_name}@{service_name}.dfs.core.windows.net` | `{path}` | `abfss://raw@mydatalake.dfs.core.windows.net` / `retail/orders/2026/07/10/` |
| **AWS S3** | `s3://{bucket_name}` | `{object_key}` | `s3://dataq-lake` / `retail/purchase_orders/part-00000.parquet` |
| **Apache Iceberg — generic REST catalog** | the catalog's raw REST URI, taken verbatim from `catalog.uri` config (no `iceberg://` prefix; whatever scheme+host the REST endpoint has) | `{iceberg_namespace}.{table}` (Iceberg's own multi-level namespace, dot-joined, exactly as Spark's `Identifier.toString()` renders it) | `https://iceberg-rest.example.com:8181` / `retail.purchase_orders` |
| **Apache Iceberg — Snowflake-hosted Iceberg REST catalog** (special-cased) | `snowflake://{account_identifier}` — reuses the Snowflake namespace rule above, not a generic REST namespace | `{warehouse}.{table}` where `warehouse` = the Iceberg `CatalogProperties.WAREHOUSE_LOCATION` conf, and the whole name is **upper-cased** | `snowflake://myorg-myaccount` / `RETAIL_WH.PURCHASE_ORDERS` |
| **dbt on Snowflake (openlineage-dbt / dbt-ol)** | `snowflake://{fix_account_name(profiles.yml "account")}` — identical normalization function to the spec's account-identifier rule | `{database}.{schema}.{table_or_alias}` (dot-join of non-empty parts, using dbt manifest's own casing) | `snowflake://myorg-myaccount` / `ANALYTICS.MARTS.FCT_ORDERS` |

Sources: spec table — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md (rendered: https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming) · Python reference impl — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/python/src/openlineage/client/naming/dataset.py · Java reference impl — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/dataset/Naming.java · Snowflake account normalization — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/utils/SnowflakeUtils.java and https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py · Unity Catalog Spark handler + its unit test (asserts scheme dropped, path dropped, host:port kept, http/https collapse to the same namespace) — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandler.java and https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/test/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandlerNamingTest.java · Iceberg catalog-type handlers — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark3/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark3/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/iceberg/RestCatalogTypeHandler.java , .../BaseCatalogTypeHandler.java , and .../SnowflakeCatalogTypeHandler.java · dbt namespace/name derivation — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py (methods `extract_namespace` and `_format_dataset_name`) · dbt integration overview — https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/

**For reference, the other datasource types in the same spec table** (useful context, not asked for directly but confirms the pattern family): BigQuery `bigquery` / `{project}.{dataset}.{table}`; Redshift `redshift://{cluster}.{region}:{port}` / `{database}.{schema}.{table}`; Postgres/MSSQL/Oracle/DB2/CrateDB same 3-part shape; MySQL/Hive/Teradata/OceanBase drop schema entirely (`{database}.{table}` — these engines have no schema level); Trino `trino://{host}:{port}` / `{catalog}.{schema}.{table}`; GCS `gs://{bucket}` / `{object_key}`; HDFS `hdfs://{namenode_host}:{namenode_port}` / `{path}`; local filesystem `file` / `{path}`; remote filesystem `file://{host}` / `{path}`; Kafka `kafka://{bootstrap_host}:{port}` / `{topic}`; PubSub `pubsub` / `topic:{projectId}:{topicId}`.

### Case sensitivity, quoting, and default-schema omission

- **No spec-level case-folding rule exists.** The name components are simply whatever the source system reports (e.g., dbt's `manifest.json` `database`/`schema`/`alias` fields, taken as-is). Practically, this means Snowflake-backed datasets will render **upper-case** unless the underlying objects were created with quoted mixed/lower-case identifiers, because Snowflake itself upper-cases unquoted identifiers — the OpenLineage layer does not re-case anything.
- **Quoting is stripped, not preserved.** `SnowflakeUtils.stripQuotes()` explicitly removes surrounding double-quotes from identifiers before they go into a dataset name/namespace, with the documented rationale: *"double quotes are used as delimiters to preserve case sensitivity or allow special characters in identifiers... quotes are not part of the actual identifier name and should be removed for normalized dataset and column names."* (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/utils/SnowflakeUtils.java)
- **Schema/database omission is engine-driven, not a generic spec default.** The spec doesn't have a universal "default schema" rule — instead, each per-engine name template is fixed at either 2 or 3 segments matching that engine's actual object hierarchy (MySQL/Hive/Teradata have no schema concept, so their template is literally 2-segment). Where a value legitimately can be missing (Snowflake account locator's region/cloud), the account-normalization function inserts an explicit default rather than omitting the segment: bare locator `xy12345` → `xy12345.us-west-1.aws` (defaults to AWS US-West Oregon); `xy12345.us-east-1` → `xy12345.us-east-1.aws` (defaults cloud to `aws`). Source: docstrings in `SnowflakeUtils.parseAccountIdentifier` / `fix_account_name` (both links above).

---

## 3. Gotchas — cross-environment ambiguity

**Yes — different Snowflake accounts (DEV vs QA) are, by design, distinct namespaces, hence distinct dataset identities.** This isn't just an inference; it's explicit in the spec text itself:

> "Snowflake is a special case where we don't have a single standardized namespace, due to its account identifier model... If you switch formats later, existing lineage nodes won't connect to new ones."
> — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md

The maintainer's design rationale (from a live GitHub discussion questioning exactly this kind of ambiguity) draws the line explicitly on **physical/tenant isolation**:

> BigQuery gets a single global namespace (`bigquery`, no project ID) because *"you can query other projects from your project: `SELECT ... FROM project.dataset.table`"* — projects are tenants inside one addressable system. AWS Glue and Spanner *do* fold the account/project ID into the namespace because they're *"physically separate systems"* with regional/instance-level isolation.
> — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/discussions/4251 (maintainer `mobuchowski`)

A DEV Snowflake account and a QA Snowflake account are exactly this second case — physically separate authentication/compute realms reachable at different hostnames — so under the spec's own logic they are *correctly* distinct namespaces (`snowflake://dev-org-account` vs `snowflake://qa-org-account`), not a modeling bug.

**What practitioners do to group "the same logical asset" across environments — the spec deliberately does not solve this at the identifier level:**
- **`SymlinksDatasetFacet`** — the one first-party mechanism for "this is also known as X" — but its documented use case is engine-level aliasing (Hive table addressed by file path vs metastore name; the same dataset seen via FQDN vs short hostname), *not* cross-environment/cross-tenant grouping. Schema: `identifiers: [{namespace, name, type}]`. Source: https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/SymlinksDatasetFacet.json (rendered: https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/facets/dataset-facets/symlinks/)
- **Custom/consumer-side facets or tags** — the discussion thread's maintainer endorses solving analogous ambiguity (MySQL across multiple hosts/datacenters) via either (a) folding a discriminator into the host-naming strategy itself, or (b) a facet-based extension, calling both "valid" — i.e., there's no canonical spec answer, it's pushed to the implementer.
- **Downstream lineage/catalog tools do the grouping**, not OpenLineage identifiers themselves — e.g., matching same `{schema}.{table}` name across differing namespaces under one business-glossary/logical-asset entity, or tagging datasets with an `environment` custom facet/tag that a UI groups on. This is consistent with `TagsDatasetFacet` existing as a generic escape hatch (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/TagsDatasetFacet.json) but there is no spec-blessed "environment" or "logical-asset-id" facet as of this research.

**Design-doc implication for DataQ:** if DataQ wants "flag the same suite/check across DEV/QA/UAT Snowflake" to read as one logical asset in any OpenLineage-facing surface, that grouping needs to live in DataQ's own domain model (e.g., a `logical_asset` tag/facet DataQ attaches), not in the OpenLineage namespace/name — the namespace will correctly and unavoidably differ per account.

---

## 4. File-pattern datasets (directories, daily-partitioned CSVs, globs)

The spec itself only goes as far as: for every blob/file-store backend (S3, GCS, ABFSS, HDFS, local, remote filesystem, WASBS), **name = `{path}`**, full stop — there is no spec-level notion of a glob or partition wildcard in the name template. Confirmed by re-checking every storage row in the canonical table (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md) and by grepping the full facets registry (`spec/facets/*.json`, https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/tree/main/spec/facets) — there is **no `PartitionDatasetFacet`** in the first-party facet set.

The convention that fills this gap comes from how the reference Spark integration actually resolves a dataset identifier for a partitioned/pattern read: it uses the relation's/table's base **`location`** (the directory root Spark/Hive resolved the partitioned table or file-source read against), not per-partition-file paths — I confirmed this by reading `PathUtils.java` (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/shared/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark/agent/util/PathUtils.java), which contains no partition- or glob-splitting logic at all; the base directory is what gets passed in as `path` for the `S3`/`HDFS`/`GCS`/`ABFSS` naming classes shown above. So the practitioner/implementation convention is exactly what you hypothesized:

- **Dataset = the directory/prefix** (e.g. `s3://dataq-lake` / `retail/purchase_orders/` — not `retail/purchase_orders/dt=2026-07-10/part-00000.csv`).
- **Partition structure is not modeled as separate datasets.** The two facets available to describe *how* that directory is physically laid out are:
  - `StorageDatasetFacet` — `storageLayer` (`iceberg`/`delta`) + `fileFormat` (`parquet`/`orc`/`avro`/`json`/`csv`/`text`/`xml`) — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/StorageDatasetFacet.json
  - `SchemaDatasetFacet` — partition *columns* (e.g. a Hive-style `dt` column) show up here as ordinary schema fields, not as a separate partition concept.
- There is no first-party facet for "list of partition values covered by this run" — teams that need that level of granularity either encode it in a custom facet or in `OutputStatisticsOutputDatasetFacet`/row-count-style facets, outside the core naming spec.

---

## 5. dbt ↔ OpenLineage (openlineage-dbt / dbt-ol) — exact naming behavior for Snowflake, verified against source

I read the actual method bodies in `integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py` (the shared processor both `dbt-ol` and the Airflow dbt-Cloud/Core operators build on) rather than relying on docs, since the docs page (https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/) doesn't spell this out.

**Namespace** — `DbtArtifactProcessor.extract_namespace(profile)`:
```python
if self.adapter_type == Adapter.SNOWFLAKE:
    return f"snowflake://{fix_account_name(profile['account'])}"
```
`profile['account']` is read straight out of the user's `profiles.yml` target block (dbt's own `account:` key), and `fix_account_name` (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py) is the **identical** normalization routine used by the core Snowflake naming spec: if the account string contains a hyphen it's treated as already being `orgname-accountname` and passed through unchanged; otherwise it's treated as a legacy locator and gets `.us-west-1.aws` appended (bare locator) or `.aws` appended (locator+region only). This is exactly the same defaulting behavior documented on the canonical Snowflake namespace row.

**Name** — `DbtArtifactProcessor._format_dataset_name(database, schema, table)`:
```python
def _format_dataset_name(self, database, schema, table):
    return ".".join(list(filter(None, [database, schema, table])))
```
called with `node.metadata_node["database"]`, `["schema"]`, and (for models) `["alias"]` or (for sources) `["name"]` — i.e. taken verbatim, in whatever case, from dbt's own `manifest.json`. No re-casing, no quote-stripping happens on the dbt side (unlike the Java `SnowflakeUtils.stripQuotes` used elsewhere in the client).

**Practical implication for matching byte-for-byte:** for DataQ's Snowflake connector to emit dataset identifiers that line up with what `dbt-ol` would emit for the *same* physical tables, it must (a) run the identical account-string normalization (hyphenated org-account passthrough vs. legacy-locator defaulting to `us-west-1`/`aws`), and (b) use the database/schema/table names exactly as Snowflake's catalog returns them unquoted (which will be upper-case for the overwhelming majority of real warehouses) rather than lower-casing or otherwise normalizing them.

Sources: https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py · https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py · https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/ (env-var config surface, e.g. `OPENLINEAGE_NAMESPACE` for job namespace, `OPENLINEAGE_DBT_USE_EXTENDED_JOB_NAME` for job *name* — neither affects dataset namespace/name, which is always profile/manifest-derived per the above).

---

<result># OpenLineage Dataset Naming Specification — Research Report

**Primary source note:** the canonical naming spec lives at `website/docs/spec/naming.md` in the OpenLineage repo, rendered at **https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming**. The old `spec/Naming.md` file you pointed at is explicitly marked obsolete — I fetched it directly and its entire content is now just a pointer:

> "This document is obsolete, please look at the [OpenLineage Naming Conventions](https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming)"
> — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/Naming.md

All namespace/name templates below are taken verbatim from the current spec table, then cross-checked against the actual reference implementations (`client/python/src/openlineage/client/naming/dataset.py` and `client/java/.../dataset/Naming.java`), which are attr/Builder classes whose `get_namespace()`/`get_name()` implementations are byte-identical to the spec table — i.e., these two files *are* the spec, expressed as code.

---

## 1–2. Per-datasource namespace + name construction, with case/quoting/defaults notes

| Datasource | Namespace template | Name template | Concrete example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Snowflake** | `snowflake://{organization_name}-{account_name}` (preferred, orgname-account format) **or** `snowflake://{account_locator}(.{region}).{cloud}` (legacy locator format) | `{database}.{schema}.{table}` | `snowflake://myorg-myaccount` / `MYDB.PUBLIC.ORDERS` — legacy form: `snowflake://xy12345.us-east-2.aws` / `MYDB.PUBLIC.ORDERS` |
| **Databricks Unity Catalog** | `unitycatalog://{host}` or `unitycatalog://{host}:{port}` | `{catalog}.{schema}.{table}` | `unitycatalog://adb-123456789.0.azuredatabricks.net` / `main.sales.orders` |
| **ADLS Gen2 (abfss://)** | `abfss://{container_name}@{service_name}.dfs.core.windows.net` | `{path}` | `abfss://raw@mydatalake.dfs.core.windows.net` / `retail/orders/2026/07/10/` |
| **AWS S3** | `s3://{bucket_name}` | `{object_key}` | `s3://dataq-lake` / `retail/purchase_orders/part-00000.parquet` |
| **Apache Iceberg — generic REST catalog** | the catalog's raw REST URI, taken verbatim from `catalog.uri` config (no `iceberg://` prefix; whatever scheme+host the REST endpoint has) | `{iceberg_namespace}.{table}` (Iceberg's own multi-level namespace, dot-joined, exactly as Spark's `Identifier.toString()` renders it) | `https://iceberg-rest.example.com:8181` / `retail.purchase_orders` |
| **Apache Iceberg — Snowflake-hosted Iceberg REST catalog** (special-cased) | `snowflake://{account_identifier}` — reuses the Snowflake namespace rule above, not a generic REST namespace | `{warehouse}.{table}` where `warehouse` = the Iceberg `CatalogProperties.WAREHOUSE_LOCATION` conf, and the whole name is **upper-cased** | `snowflake://myorg-myaccount` / `RETAIL_WH.PURCHASE_ORDERS` |
| **dbt on Snowflake (openlineage-dbt / dbt-ol)** | `snowflake://{fix_account_name(profiles.yml "account")}` — identical normalization function to the spec's account-identifier rule | `{database}.{schema}.{table_or_alias}` (dot-join of non-empty parts, using dbt manifest's own casing) | `snowflake://myorg-myaccount` / `ANALYTICS.MARTS.FCT_ORDERS` |

Sources: spec table — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md (rendered: https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming) · Python reference impl — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/python/src/openlineage/client/naming/dataset.py · Java reference impl — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/dataset/Naming.java · Snowflake account normalization — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/utils/SnowflakeUtils.java and https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py · Unity Catalog Spark handler + its unit test (asserts scheme dropped, path dropped, host:port kept, http/https collapse to the same namespace) — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandler.java and https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/test/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandlerNamingTest.java · Iceberg catalog-type handlers — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark3/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark3/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/iceberg/RestCatalogTypeHandler.java , .../BaseCatalogTypeHandler.java , and .../SnowflakeCatalogTypeHandler.java · dbt namespace/name derivation — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py (methods `extract_namespace` and `_format_dataset_name`) · dbt integration overview — https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/

**For reference, the other datasource types in the same spec table** (useful context, not asked for directly but confirms the pattern family): BigQuery `bigquery` / `{project}.{dataset}.{table}`; Redshift `redshift://{cluster}.{region}:{port}` / `{database}.{schema}.{table}`; Postgres/MSSQL/Oracle/DB2/CrateDB same 3-part shape; MySQL/Hive/Teradata/OceanBase drop schema entirely (`{database}.{table}` — these engines have no schema level); Trino `trino://{host}:{port}` / `{catalog}.{schema}.{table}`; GCS `gs://{bucket}` / `{object_key}`; HDFS `hdfs://{namenode_host}:{namenode_port}` / `{path}`; local filesystem `file` / `{path}`; remote filesystem `file://{host}` / `{path}`; Kafka `kafka://{bootstrap_host}:{port}` / `{topic}`; PubSub `pubsub` / `topic:{projectId}:{topicId}`.

### Case sensitivity, quoting, and default-schema omission

- **No spec-level case-folding rule exists.** The name components are simply whatever the source system reports (e.g., dbt's `manifest.json` `database`/`schema`/`alias` fields, taken as-is). Practically, this means Snowflake-backed datasets will render **upper-case** unless the underlying objects were created with quoted mixed/lower-case identifiers, because Snowflake itself upper-cases unquoted identifiers — the OpenLineage layer does not re-case anything.
- **Quoting is stripped, not preserved.** `SnowflakeUtils.stripQuotes()` explicitly removes surrounding double-quotes from identifiers before they go into a dataset name/namespace, with the documented rationale: *"double quotes are used as delimiters to preserve case sensitivity or allow special characters in identifiers... quotes are not part of the actual identifier name and should be removed for normalized dataset and column names."* (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/utils/SnowflakeUtils.java)
- **Schema/database omission is engine-driven, not a generic spec default.** The spec doesn't have a universal "default schema" rule — instead, each per-engine name template is fixed at either 2 or 3 segments matching that engine's actual object hierarchy (MySQL/Hive/Teradata have no schema concept, so their template is literally 2-segment). Where a value legitimately can be missing (Snowflake account locator's region/cloud), the account-normalization function inserts an explicit default rather than omitting the segment: bare locator `xy12345` → `xy12345.us-west-1.aws` (defaults to AWS US-West Oregon); `xy12345.us-east-1` → `xy12345.us-east-1.aws` (defaults cloud to `aws`). Source: docstrings in `SnowflakeUtils.parseAccountIdentifier` / `fix_account_name` (both links above).

---

## 3. Gotchas — cross-environment ambiguity

**Yes — different Snowflake accounts (DEV vs QA) are, by design, distinct namespaces, hence distinct dataset identities.** This isn't just an inference; it's explicit in the spec text itself:

> "Snowflake is a special case where we don't have a single standardized namespace, due to its account identifier model... If you switch formats later, existing lineage nodes won't connect to new ones."
> — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md

The maintainer's design rationale (from a live GitHub discussion questioning exactly this kind of ambiguity) draws the line explicitly on **physical/tenant isolation**:

> BigQuery gets a single global namespace (`bigquery`, no project ID) because *"you can query other projects from your project: `SELECT ... FROM project.dataset.table`"* — projects are tenants inside one addressable system. AWS Glue and Spanner *do* fold the account/project ID into the namespace because they're *"physically separate systems"* with regional/instance-level isolation.
> — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/discussions/4251 (maintainer `mobuchowski`)

A DEV Snowflake account and a QA Snowflake account are exactly this second case — physically separate authentication/compute realms reachable at different hostnames — so under the spec's own logic they are *correctly* distinct namespaces (`snowflake://dev-org-account` vs `snowflake://qa-org-account`), not a modeling bug.

**What practitioners do to group "the same logical asset" across environments — the spec deliberately does not solve this at the identifier level:**
- **`SymlinksDatasetFacet`** — the one first-party mechanism for "this is also known as X" — but its documented use case is engine-level aliasing (Hive table addressed by file path vs metastore name; the same dataset seen via FQDN vs short hostname), *not* cross-environment/cross-tenant grouping. Schema: `identifiers: [{namespace, name, type}]`. Source: https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/SymlinksDatasetFacet.json (rendered: https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/facets/dataset-facets/symlinks/)
- **Custom/consumer-side facets or tags** — the discussion thread's maintainer endorses solving analogous ambiguity (MySQL across multiple hosts/datacenters) via either (a) folding a discriminator into the host-naming strategy itself, or (b) a facet-based extension, calling both "valid" — i.e., there's no canonical spec answer, it's pushed to the implementer.
- **Downstream lineage/catalog tools do the grouping**, not OpenLineage identifiers themselves — e.g., matching same `{schema}.{table}` name across differing namespaces under one business-glossary/logical-asset entity, or tagging datasets with an `environment` custom facet/tag that a UI groups on. This is consistent with `TagsDatasetFacet` existing as a generic escape hatch (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/TagsDatasetFacet.json) but there is no spec-blessed "environment" or "logical-asset-id" facet as of this research.

**Design-doc implication for DataQ:** if DataQ wants "flag the same suite/check across DEV/QA/UAT Snowflake" to read as one logical asset in any OpenLineage-facing surface, that grouping needs to live in DataQ's own domain model (e.g., a `logical_asset` tag/facet DataQ attaches), not in the OpenLineage namespace/name — the namespace will correctly and unavoidably differ per account.

---

## 4. File-pattern datasets (directories, daily-partitioned CSVs, globs)

The spec itself only goes as far as: for every blob/file-store backend (S3, GCS, ABFSS, HDFS, local, remote filesystem, WASBS), **name = `{path}`**, full stop — there is no spec-level notion of a glob or partition wildcard in the name template. Confirmed by re-checking every storage row in the canonical table (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md) and by grepping the full facets registry (`spec/facets/*.json`, https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/tree/main/spec/facets) — there is **no `PartitionDatasetFacet`** in the first-party facet set.

The convention that fills this gap comes from how the reference Spark integration actually resolves a dataset identifier for a partitioned/pattern read: it uses the relation's/table's base **`location`** (the directory root Spark/Hive resolved the partitioned table or file-source read against), not per-partition-file paths — I confirmed this by reading `PathUtils.java` (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/shared/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark/agent/util/PathUtils.java), which contains no partition- or glob-splitting logic at all; the base directory is what gets passed in as `path` for the `S3`/`HDFS`/`GCS`/`ABFSS` naming classes shown above. So the practitioner/implementation convention is exactly what you hypothesized:

- **Dataset = the directory/prefix** (e.g. `s3://dataq-lake` / `retail/purchase_orders/` — not `retail/purchase_orders/dt=2026-07-10/part-00000.csv`).
- **Partition structure is not modeled as separate datasets.** The two facets available to describe *how* that directory is physically laid out are:
  - `StorageDatasetFacet` — `storageLayer` (`iceberg`/`delta`) + `fileFormat` (`parquet`/`orc`/`avro`/`json`/`csv`/`text`/`xml`) — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/StorageDatasetFacet.json
  - `SchemaDatasetFacet` — partition *columns* (e.g. a Hive-style `dt` column) show up here as ordinary schema fields, not as a separate partition concept.
- There is no first-party facet for "list of partition values covered by this run" — teams that need that level of granularity either encode it in a custom facet or in `OutputStatisticsOutputDatasetFacet`/row-count-style facets, outside the core naming spec.

---

## 5. dbt ↔ OpenLineage (openlineage-dbt / dbt-ol) — exact naming behavior for Snowflake, verified against source

I read the actual method bodies in `integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py` (the shared processor both `dbt-ol` and the Airflow dbt-Cloud/Core operators build on) rather than relying on docs, since the docs page (https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/) doesn't spell this out.

**Namespace** — `DbtArtifactProcessor.extract_namespace(profile)`:
```python
if self.adapter_type == Adapter.SNOWFLAKE:
    return f"snowflake://{fix_account_name(profile['account'])}"
```
`profile['account']` is read straight out of the user's `profiles.yml` target block (dbt's own `account:` key), and `fix_account_name` (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py) is the **identical** normalization routine used by the core Snowflake naming spec: if the account string contains a hyphen it's treated as already being `orgname-accountname` and passed through unchanged; otherwise it's treated as a legacy locator and gets `.us-west-1.aws` appended (bare locator) or `.aws` appended (locator+region only). This is exactly the same defaulting behavior documented on the canonical Snowflake namespace row.

**Name** — `DbtArtifactProcessor._format_dataset_name(database, schema, table)`:
```python
def _format_dataset_name(self, database, schema, table):
    return ".".join(list(filter(None, [database, schema, table])))
```
called with `node.metadata_node["database"]`, `["schema"]`, and (for models) `["alias"]` or (for sources) `["name"]` — i.e. taken verbatim, in whatever case, from dbt's own `manifest.json`. No re-casing, no quote-stripping happens on the dbt side (unlike the Java `SnowflakeUtils.stripQuotes` used elsewhere in the client).

**Practical implication for matching byte-for-byte:** for DataQ's Snowflake connector to emit dataset identifiers that line up with what `dbt-ol` would emit for the *same* physical tables, it must (a) run the identical account-string normalization (hyphenated org-account passthrough vs. legacy-locator defaulting to `us-west-1`/`aws`), and (b) use the database/schema/table names exactly as Snowflake's catalog returns them unquoted (which will be upper-case for the overwhelming majority of real warehouses) rather than lower-casing or otherwise normalizing them.

Sources: https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py · https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py · https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/ (env-var config surface, e.g. `OPENLINEAGE_NAMESPACE` for job namespace, `OPENLINEAGE_DBT_USE_EXTENDED_JOB_NAME` for job *name* — neither affects dataset namespace/name, which is always profile/manifest-derived per the above).

---

### Full list of primary sources cited above
- https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/Naming.md (obsolete redirect)
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/python/src/openlineage/client/naming/dataset.py

- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/local.py
- https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandler.java

content.text:
All four research reports are in — I have everything needed to draft. Quick recap of the last one: the OpenLineage naming spec gives exact namespace/name templates for all five of our datasource types, confirms DEV/QA accounts are *correctly* distinct identities (logical grouping is our job, via our own domain model), file-pattern assets = the directory/prefix, and the exact dbt-ol normalization rules to match byte-for-byte.

Now drafting. Let me set up the branch and skim a sibling doc for voice.

1	# Post-v1 notes — Expectation expansion, marketplace & LLM-assisted authoring (deferred design)
2	
3	> **Status: deferred to post-v1.** Captured so the design intent isn't lost. None of
4	> this is a v1 blocker — the v1 DQ loop (checks → results → trends → freshness/volume
5	> monitors → alerts → MCP) stands on its own. These are the "intelligence" layer on
6	> top of it.
7	>
8	> **The big enabler (already true in v1):** there is **no server-side expectation
9	> allowlist** — [`gx_runner`](datasources/gx_runner.py) title-cases *any*
10	> `expectation_type` string into a GX class, and `checks.config` is free-form JSONB.
11	> So "add an expectation" / "wire in a check" is mostly a **frontend-catalog + config-
12	> validation** problem, not a backend-engine one. That's why the items below are
13	> cheaper than they look.
14	
15	## The four themes
16	
17	### 1. Implement the 5 high-ROI GX built-ins
18	**Feasibility: High · Effort: S–M · No new infra.**
19	
20	- **4 of 5 are catalog-only** entries in
21	  [`frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts`](../frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts):
22	  - **`mostly`** — tolerance on any existing expectation ("95% not-null"). One optional
23	    field; GX still emits `unexpected_percent`, so it bands under ADR 0016 unchanged.
24	    **Highest ROI / lowest cost** — do first.
25	  - **Compound / cross-column** — `expect_compound_columns_to_be_unique` (multi-column
26	    primary keys), `expect_column_pair_values_a_to_be_greater_than_b` (e.g.
27	    `end_date > start_date`), `expect_multicolumn_sum_to_equal`.
28	  - **Type** — `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type` / `in_type_list`.
29	  - **Set relations / date format** — `expect_column_distinct_values_to_be_in_set` /
30	    `to_contain_set`, `expect_column_values_to_match_strftime_format`.
31	- **The 5th — aggregate stats** (`expect_column_{mean,median,sum,stdev,min,max}_to_be_between`)
32	  is the **design decision, not a free add.** They don't emit `unexpected_percent`; the
33	  result is a **scalar in `observed_value`** and they're **two-sided** (too low *or* too
34	  high fails). That's the *volume-monitor* shape, not the GX unexpected-% shape — so
35	  aggregates likely belong on the **monitor-kind `metric_value` path** (ADR 0012, the
36	  freshness/volume engine, #426) as a 3rd monitor kind, **not** as a GX-banded
37	  expectation. Decide deliberately before building.
38	
39	### 2. LLM SQL generator for custom-SQL checks (`UnexpectedRowsExpectation`)
40	**Feasibility: High · Effort: M · Best-aligned of the four.**
41	
42	- NL rule → LLM-generated SQL → existing **custom-SQL guardrails** (ADR 0019:
43	  read-only single-statement validation + SQL-datasource gating + least-privilege role)
44	  → **dry-run preview** before save.
45	- **Principle: trust LLM SQL no more than user SQL** — run it through the *same*
46	  validator + dry-run. That neutralizes "LLM writes `DROP TABLE`" for free.
47	- Real risk is *correctness* (hallucinated columns), mitigated by feeding **schema +
48	  column-profiler stats** as context (we already have the profiler).
49	
50	### 3. LLM curated check suggestions for a suite
51	**Feasibility: High · Effort: M.**
52	
53	- "GX Data Assistant, but LLM-driven and constrained to *our* catalog vocabulary." The
54	  input it needs already exists — the **column profiler** (null %, distinct counts,
55	  distributions).
56	- Must use **structured / schema-constrained output**: the LLM emits checks in the
57	  catalog's exact `expectation_type` + config schema (derive a tool schema from the
58	  catalog), so it can't suggest something the runner can't run. Pairs with #1 (richer
59	  catalog ⇒ better suggestions) and the MCP work.
60	
61	### 4. Plug-and-play expectation marketplace — **scope = 4a (curated superset)**
62	**Feasibility: High.**
63	
64	- **Chosen (4a): a vetted, server-served superset of GX's ~50 built-ins** — the existing
65	  catalog pattern scaled up. The frontend picks; the backend already runs it.
66	- **Not doing (4b): an open marketplace** of community/contrib or arbitrary custom
67	  `Expectation` classes. "Backend wires it in" from user input = a **code-execution /
68	  supply-chain risk**; such classes would have to be installed in the image + pinned +
69	  reviewed, not loaded dynamically. Against the anti-supply-chain posture.
70	- **Useful irony:** doing 4a *safely* means **adding the server-side allowlist** v1
71	  lacks — flip the "no allowlist" convenience into a vetted catalog the backend validates
72	  submitted `expectation_type`s against. (This also hardens #2/#3's generated output.)
73	
74	## Sequencing
75	
76	```
77	#1 built-ins ──► richer vocabulary ──► #3 suggestions
78	   (cheap, now-ish)                        ▲
79	                                           │ both need:
80	#4a curated marketplace (extends #1)       │
81	                              LLM-client seam (NEW) ──► #2 SQL-gen
82	```
83	
84	Suggested order: **#1** (immediate value, no infra, unblocks #3's vocabulary) → **#4a**
85	(extends #1; adds the validation allowlist) → **LLM seam + #2** (highest user "wow,"
86	guardrails already done) → **#3** (needs the seam *and* the richer catalog).
87	
88	## LLM integration model (proposed — not locked)
89	
90	**#2 and #3 share one capability v1 doesn't have: DataQ calling an LLM *outbound*.**
91	(Note the W7 **MCP server is the opposite direction** — it exposes *our* tools to
92	external LLM clients; it does not let us call one.) The recommended shape mirrors the
93	seams the app already has (`ConnectionAdapter`, `SecretStore`, `OrchestrationProvider`):
94	
95	**An `LLMProvider` seam — admin-configured, default-OFF, customer brings their own
96	credential/endpoint.**
97	
98	- **Configuration scope = workspace-admin, not per-user.** Single-tenant + customer-
99	  deployed (BYOL, ADR 0013): the admin (`WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS`) configures **one**
100	  provider (provider + endpoint + model + credential), credential stored in the
101	  **`SecretStore`** (Key Vault is one impl). Per-user keys are friction for a team tool —
102	  defer as an optional power-user *override*, not the primary model.
103	- **Default off + graceful degradation.** The app must fully work with no LLM configured
104	  (hand-authored checks, the catalog, custom SQL). LLM features are additive.
105	- **Pluggable concrete impls behind the seam (anti-lock-in, ADR 0010/0013 — no hardcoded
106	  vendor in business logic):**
107	  1. **Anthropic API** — default impl (CLAUDE.md: default to the latest Claude models).
108	  2. **Azure AI Foundry / Azure OpenAI** — for customers already on Azure.
109	  3. **AWS Bedrock** — for customers on AWS.
110	  4. **Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint** — this is the **"local LLM" answer**: we do
111	     **not** bake a model server or HF download into the app image (against the slim-image
112	     ADR 0025 — GPU/ops/size). Instead the customer runs their own inference server
113	     (**Ollama / vLLM / HF TGI**, which is where they'd pull an HF model) and points
114	     DataQ at its OpenAI-compatible URL. "Local LLM" = an endpoint impl, uniform with the
115	     others.
116	  - Most of these speak OpenAI-compatible or a thin adapter, so the seam is largely
117	    `{base_url, api_key, model, auth_adapter}` + a capability flag for tool-use/structured
118	    output (some local models do JSON-schema output poorly → prompt-based JSON fallback).
119	- **Cost ownership = the customer's.** BYOL means the customer pays their own LLM bill via
120	  their own key/endpoint. **Never proxy through an Anthropic key we own** — that turns it
121	  into hosted SaaS + our cost (contra ADR 0013).
122	- **Data residency is the deciding axis** for *which* provider a customer picks, and it's
123	  a hard requirement: apply the **same PII discipline** as the rest of the app — send the
124	  LLM **schema + aggregate profiler stats only, never raw sample rows / PII-column
125	  values** (reuse the redaction seam). The local-endpoint impl exists precisely for
126	  customers who can't send even schema externally.
127	- **Where it runs:** the **Celery worker** (async, already holds secret access) — not the
128	  request path. An ADR should record the seam + default impl + the "BYO credential,
129	  default-off, no-proxy" posture.
130	
131	## Guiding principle
132	
133	Build the intelligence layer **on top of** a solid DQ loop, constrained to the catalog
134	vocabulary and the existing safety seams (custom-SQL read-only validation, PII redaction,
135	SecretStore, anti-lock-in). The LLM is an **authoring assistant**, never an unchecked
136	execution path — generated SQL and suggested checks go through the **same** validation a
137	human's would.
138

# Post-v1 notes — Expectation expansion, marketplace & LLM-assisted authoring (deferred design)

> **Status: deferred to post-v1.** Captured so the design intent isn't lost. None of
> this is a v1 blocker — the v1 DQ loop (checks → results → trends → freshness/volume
> monitors → alerts → MCP) stands on its own. These are the "intelligence" layer on
> top of it.
>
> **The big enabler (already true in v1):** there is **no server-side expectation
> allowlist** — [`gx_runner`](datasources/gx_runner.py) title-cases *any*
> `expectation_type` string into a GX class, and `checks.config` is free-form JSONB.
> So "add an expectation" / "wire in a check" is mostly a **frontend-catalog + config-
> validation** problem, not a backend-engine one. That's why the items below are
> cheaper than they look.

## The four themes

### 1. Implement the 5 high-ROI GX built-ins
**Feasibility: High · Effort: S–M · No new infra.**

- **4 of 5 are catalog-only** entries in
  [`frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts`](../frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts):
  - **`mostly`** — tolerance on any existing expectation ("95% not-null"). One optional
    field; GX still emits `unexpected_percent`, so it bands under ADR 0016 unchanged.
    **Highest ROI / lowest cost** — do first.
  - **Compound / cross-column** — `expect_compound_columns_to_be_unique` (multi-column
    primary keys), `expect_column_pair_values_a_to_be_greater_than_b` (e.g.
    `end_date > start_date`), `expect_multicolumn_sum_to_equal`.
  - **Type** — `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type` / `in_type_list`.
  - **Set relations / date format** — `expect_column_distinct_values_to_be_in_set` /
    `to_contain_set`, `expect_column_values_to_match_strftime_format`.
- **The 5th — aggregate stats** (`expect_column_{mean,median,sum,stdev,min,max}_to_be_between`)
  is the **design decision, not a free add.** They don't emit `unexpected_percent`; the
  result is a **scalar in `observed_value`** and they're **two-sided** (too low *or* too
  high fails). That's the *volume-monitor* shape, not the GX unexpected-% shape — so
  aggregates likely belong on the **monitor-kind `metric_value` path** (ADR 0012, the
  freshness/volume engine, #426) as a 3rd monitor kind, **not** as a GX-banded
  expectation. Decide deliberately before building.

### 2. LLM SQL generator for custom-SQL checks (`UnexpectedRowsExpectation`)
**Feasibility: High · Effort: M · Best-aligned of the four.**

- NL rule → LLM-generated SQL → existing **custom-SQL guardrails** (ADR 0019:
  read-only single-statement validation + SQL-datasource gating + least-privilege role)
  → **dry-run preview** before save.
- **Principle: trust LLM SQL no more than user SQL** — run it through the *same*
  validator + dry-run. That neutralizes "LLM writes `DROP TABLE`" for free.
- Real risk is *correctness* (hallucinated columns), mitigated by feeding **schema +
  column-profiler stats** as context (we already have the profiler).

### 3. LLM curated check suggestions for a suite
**Feasibility: High · Effort: M.**

- "GX Data Assistant, but LLM-driven and constrained to *our* catalog vocabulary." The
  input it needs already exists — the **column profiler** (null %, distinct counts,
  distributions).
- Must use **structured / schema-constrained output**: the LLM emits checks in the
  catalog's exact `expectation_type` + config schema (derive a tool schema from the
  catalog), so it can't suggest something the runner can't run. Pairs with #1 (richer
  catalog ⇒ better suggestions) and the MCP work.

### 4. Plug-and-play expectation marketplace — **scope = 4a (curated superset)**
**Feasibility: High.**

- **Chosen (4a): a vetted, server-served superset of GX's ~50 built-ins** — the existing
  catalog pattern scaled up. The frontend picks; the backend already runs it.
- **Not doing (4b): an open marketplace** of community/contrib or arbitrary custom
  `Expectation` classes. "Backend wires it in" from user input = a **code-execution /
  supply-chain risk**; such classes would have to be installed in the image + pinned +
  reviewed, not loaded dynamically. Against the anti-supply-chain posture.
- **Useful irony:** doing 4a *safely* means **adding the server-side allowlist** v1
  lacks — flip the "no allowlist" convenience into a vetted catalog the backend validates
  submitted `expectation_type`s against. (This also hardens #2/#3's generated output.)

## Sequencing

```
#1 built-ins ──► richer vocabulary ──► #3 suggestions
   (cheap, now-ish)                        ▲
                                           │ both need:
#4a curated marketplace (extends #1)       │
                              LLM-client seam (NEW) ──► #2 SQL-gen
```

Suggested order: **#1** (immediate value, no infra, unblocks #3's vocabulary) → **#4a**
(extends #1; adds the validation allowlist) → **LLM seam + #2** (highest user "wow,"
guardrails already done) → **#3** (needs the seam *and* the richer catalog).

## LLM integration model (proposed — not locked)

**#2 and #3 share one capability v1 doesn't have: DataQ calling an LLM *outbound*.**
(Note the W7 **MCP server is the opposite direction** — it exposes *our* tools to
external LLM clients; it does not let us call one.) The recommended shape mirrors the
seams the app already has (`ConnectionAdapter`, `SecretStore`, `OrchestrationProvider`):

**An `LLMProvider` seam — admin-configured, default-OFF, customer brings their own
credential/endpoint.**

- **Configuration scope = workspace-admin, not per-user.** Single-tenant + customer-
  deployed (BYOL, ADR 0013): the admin (`WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS`) configures **one**
  provider (provider + endpoint + model + credential), credential stored in the
  **`SecretStore`** (Key Vault is one impl). Per-user keys are friction for a team tool —
  defer as an optional power-user *override*, not the primary model.
- **Default off + graceful degradation.** The app must fully work with no LLM configured
  (hand-authored checks, the catalog, custom SQL). LLM features are additive.
- **Pluggable concrete impls behind the seam (anti-lock-in, ADR 0010/0013 — no hardcoded
  vendor in business logic):**
  1. **Anthropic API** — default impl (CLAUDE.md: default to the latest Claude models).
  2. **Azure AI Foundry / Azure OpenAI** — for customers already on Azure.
  3. **AWS Bedrock** — for customers on AWS.
  4. **Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint** — this is the **"local LLM" answer**: we do
     **not** bake a model server or HF download into the app image (against the slim-image
     ADR 0025 — GPU/ops/size). Instead the customer runs their own inference server
     (**Ollama / vLLM / HF TGI**, which is where they'd pull an HF model) and points
     DataQ at its OpenAI-compatible URL. "Local LLM" = an endpoint impl, uniform with the
     others.
  - Most of these speak OpenAI-compatible or a thin adapter, so the seam is largely
    `{base_url, api_key, model, auth_adapter}` + a capability flag for tool-use/structured
    output (some local models do JSON-schema output poorly → prompt-based JSON fallback).
- **Cost ownership = the customer's.** BYOL means the customer pays their own LLM bill via
  their own key/endpoint. **Never proxy through an Anthropic key we own** — that turns it
  into hosted SaaS + our cost (contra ADR 0013).
- **Data residency is the deciding axis** for *which* provider a customer picks, and it's
  a hard requirement: apply the **same PII discipline** as the rest of the app — send the
  LLM **schema + aggregate profiler stats only, never raw sample rows / PII-column
  values** (reuse the redaction seam). The local-endpoint impl exists precisely for
  customers who can't send even schema externally.
- **Where it runs:** the **Celery worker** (async, already holds secret access) — not the
  request path. An ADR should record the seam + default impl + the "BYO credential,
  default-off, no-proxy" posture.

## Guiding principle

Build the intelligence layer **on top of** a solid DQ loop, constrained to the catalog
vocabulary and the existing safety seams (custom-SQL read-only validation, PII redaction,
SecretStore, anti-lock-in). The LLM is an **authoring assistant**, never an unchecked
execution path — generated SQL and suggested checks go through the **same** validation a
human's would.
